title: The posture reset
goal: You can get from 'just picked it up' to 'ready to play, relaxed' in ten seconds, standing or sitting.

title: Meet your tone (ears first)
goal: You can hear the difference between plucking near the neck and near the bridge, and between tone knob up and down.

title: Match each open string to its reference
goal: Each open string rings against its reference tone with no wobble — four strings, under a minute.

title: The 5th-fret cross-check
goal: Each fretted note and the open string above it sound identical — if one pair wobbles, you know exactly which string to fix.

title: Quarters and rests, spoken and played
goal: Notes land exactly on the click, rests are exactly silent (string stopped, not fading), and your counting never pauses.

title: Eighth notes with the '&' spoken
goal: A full bar of eighths sits evenly inside the beat — no rushing the '&'s — at 80 bpm with alternating fingers.

title: The dotted-quarter + eighth (the classic)
goal: The second note lands cleanly on the '& of 2' and '& of 4' — the lopsided-on-purpose feel that powers half of pop bass.

title: Lines: see it, say it, play it
goal: You name each line note out loud a beat before you play it, in time, at 60 bpm.

title: First real reading: lines and spaces mixed
goal: Eight mixed notes read cold — no mnemonics recited, just landmarks — cleanly at 60 bpm.

title: Two-lens reading
goal: You can play the page's phrase three ways: from the stacked pair, from tab alone (recalling the rhythm), and from notation alone (choosing your own frets).

title: One phrase, two fingerings
goal: You can play D–E–G twice — once using open strings, once fully fretted at the 5th position — and hear that pitch is identical while feel differs.

title: Waltz bass: own beat 1 in 3/4
goal: Beat 1 lands like furniture being set down — solid, unhurried — while 2 and 3 stay light, for a full minute without drifting.

title: 6/8: play the two big pulses
goal: You sound only the two dotted-quarter pulses per bar while counting all six eighths out loud — the rolling feel appears by itself.

title: Root–fifth, walked up the neck
goal: The fifth shape lands without looking, from four different roots, both as up-a-fifth and down-a-fifth (same fret, string below).

title: Major vs minor third: hear the color flip
goal: From the same root, you can play both thirds back-to-back and hear (and name) happy vs. sad — from any root you pick.

title: Octave jumps in time
goal: Root–octave–root bounces cleanly (octave = 2 strings up, 2 frets over) with the octave popping out evenly, disco-style, at 90 bpm.

title: Natural notes up the E string
goal: You can play E–F–G–A–B–C–D–E up the low string, naming each note out loud, without pausing to count frets.

title: Natural notes up the A string
goal: Same drill on the A string — and you can find C (fret 3) instantly, eyes closed.

title: Octave shapes across the neck
goal: From any note on the E or A string, your hand finds the octave (two strings up, two frets over) without looking.

title: The 1-2-3-4 chromatic warmup
goal: All four fingers place notes of equal volume and length across all four strings in 3rd position — no flams, no buzzes — at 70 bpm.

title: The two-position shift
goal: The shift from 3rd to 5th position happens during the long note — silently, calmly — and the landing note speaks exactly on the beat.

title: Root motion: I–V–vi–IV in three keys
goal: Two bars per chord, roots only, in C, then G, then D — announced by numbers ('one… five… six-minor… four…') and landed without hesitation.

title: The ii–V–I with thirds
goal: Dm7–G7–Cmaj7 outlined as root+third per chord, so each chord's quality is audible from the bass alone.

title: Play–sing–check: fifths and octaves
goal: You can sing the fifth or octave above a played root before you play it — and the check note proves you right, 8 times out of 10.

title: Major and minor thirds, in time
goal: Alternating major/minor thirds from moving roots, in steady time, while calling out 'major' or 'minor' — no pausing to compute.

title: Roots around the circle
goal: You can play the circle's roots in order — C G D A E B F♯ D♭ A♭ E♭ B♭ F — two beats each, without consulting the diagram.

title: One scale shape, four keys
goal: The major-scale box lands cleanly in G, D, A, and E — proving to your hands that new sharps don't mean new fingerings.

title: One octave, degrees out loud
goal: G major up and down while saying '1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8' — then the same shape from A and from C without re-thinking the fingering.

title: Skips: 1-3, 2-4, 3-5…
goal: Playing the scale in thirds (skip one degree each time) stays clean and you can still name what you're on — the drill that turns scales into music.

title: Natural minor, one octave, degrees aloud
goal: A minor up and down, calling '1 2 ♭3 4 5 ♭6 ♭7 8' — the flats said out loud — then the same shape from E.

title: The parallel flip: A major ↔ A minor
goal: You can play both scales from the same root back-to-back and your ear tracks exactly which three notes moved (3, 6, 7 — each one fret down).

title: The box, up and down, degrees aloud
goal: A minor pentatonic box flows evenly at 90 bpm, degrees spoken ('1, ♭3, 4, 5, ♭7, 8'), from A and then E without re-thinking.

title: The ♭5 sting (pass through, don't live there)
goal: The chromatic 4–♭5–5 move slides smoothly inside a line — the ♭5 always resolving, never parked on a strong beat.

title: The four seventh arpeggios from C
goal: Cmaj7 → C7 → Cm7 → Cø7 arpeggios in sequence, one position each, naming the note that changed between neighbors.

title: Symbol flashcard sprint (no instrument)
goal: Given any of Dm7, G7, Cmaj7, Eø7, F/A, Bb7 — you can say the root, the quality, and the one note you'd play first, within two seconds each.

title: Major and minor arpeggios, one position
goal: G, then Gm, then A, then Am arpeggios (root–3rd–5th–octave–back) at 80 bpm — you always know whether your next note is the major or minor third.

title: Blind quality check (self-test)
goal: Using the demo above with eyes off the screen: 8 of 10 triad plays identified as major/minor by ear alone.

title: Run one index-card session
goal: One complete 20-minute session executed to the shape above, with a written one-line goal, and the goal met or honestly assessed.

title: Chunk surgery on a real problem
goal: One passage that currently breaks, isolated to its smallest failing piece, looped to 3× clean at a slow tempo, then reattached to its phrase.

title: The minimum-pressure test
goal: On three different frets (low, middle, high) you can find the buzz point and settle just above it — and your default grip noticeably lightens over a week.

title: Finger permutations (1234 → 1324 → 1423…)
goal: Four permutation patterns cleanly at 60 bpm, one note per click, every finger landing on its own fret with even volume.

title: Hammer-on / pull-off pairs
goal: The unplucked note is at least 80% as loud as the plucked one — hammered and pulled, on every string pair you try.

title: Pure alternation on open strings
goal: Sixteen even eighth notes per string, i-m-i-m without exception, identical volume from both fingers — recorded and verified by your own ears.

title: String crossing without hiccups
goal: Crossing E→A→D→G and back keeps the i-m alternation unbroken (no sneaky double-index at the crossings) at 80 bpm.

title: Note length on command
goal: The same four roots played twice: once ringing full value, once cut to half value by plucking-hand touch — cleanly, on time, by choice.

title: Whole notes, brutally honest
goal: Eight whole notes in a row land exactly with the click while you count '1-2-3-4' through each — the hardest easy exercise on this site.

title: Half-time click (click = 2 and 4)
goal: A one-bar root groove stays locked for two minutes with the click only on beats 2 and 4 — and it starts to feel BETTER than four clicks.

title: Eighth-note lock with accents
goal: Continuous eighths with an accent only on beat 1, then only on the '& of 2' — accents move, the grid doesn't.

title: Ten identical notes
goal: Ten plucks on one fretted note that are indistinguishable in volume, tone, and length — verified by recording, on two different strings.

title: Dynamic terraces: p → mf → f → p
goal: A one-bar root pattern played at three distinct, repeatable volumes — the quiet one still full-toned, the loud one still clean.

title: The palm-mute flip
goal: Alternating bars of ringing fingerstyle and palm-muted thump on the same line, switching cleanly in the one-beat gap.

title: Chromatic from below, everywhere
goal: G–C–D–G with the last eighth of each bar approaching the next root from one fret below — landings inevitable, approach never early.

title: Mixing the three flavors
goal: The same progression with a different approach type each pass (below, above, dominant) — chosen before the bar starts, not improvised in panic.

title: Passing tones inside the bar
goal: Root–2–3–5 through each chord (the passing 2 on the weak beat), even quarters — your first taste of a real walking line.

title: Roots on the ones (the whole job, minimal edition)
goal: Over a looped Am–F–C–G (use the demo or the groove machine later): the correct root lands exactly on beat 1 of every bar for two minutes straight.

title: Compose with silence
goal: The same Am–F–C–G loop with a one-beat rest deliberately placed in each bar — the silence landing as precisely as the notes.

title: Root–fifth heartbeat through a progression
goal: G–C–D–G with root on 1, fifth on 3 (mixing high and LOW fifths), steady for two minutes — the oldest bass line in the book, owned.

title: Spell each chord as it passes
goal: Am7–D7–Gmaj7 with R-3-5-7 arpeggios fitting exactly one bar each — the quality of every chord audible from your line alone.

title: The octave engine
goal: Root-octave eighths over Am–G–F–G, octaves popping evenly, idle strings silent (the muting test), at 100 bpm.

title: Map three songs you love
goal: Three one-page charts in your own hand: sections, bar counts, chords (or numerals), and the landmine bars circled.

title: Play 'Midnight Sandwich' from the chart
goal: The example chart above performed start to finish — repeats, D.S., coda — with roots-and-fifths, no navigation errors, twice in a row.

title: Roots through the full form (three keys)
goal: A complete 12-bar chorus from memory — no chart — with correct changes, in A, then G, then E. The form recited out loud while playing.

title: The boogie line over the whole form
goal: The R-3-5-6 pattern maintained through all 12 bars with clean shape-shifts at every chord change, at 100 bpm.

title: Three turnarounds, on demand
goal: Bar 11–12 played three ways (sit on V; walk-up A–B–C♯–E; chromatic drop) each landing the next chorus's beat 1 like an arrival, not an escape.

title: The recipe over two chords
goal: Four choruses of Dm7–G7 (one bar each), never repeating the exact same bar twice, every beat-4 aiming correctly — at even 80 bpm quarters.

title: Write one chorus (pencil, then hands)
goal: A written 4-bar walking line over Am7–D7–Gmaj7–E7 that obeys the recipe, then played from your own chart from memory.

title: Two chords per bar
goal: Dm7 G7 | Cmaj7 (the classic squeeze) with roots on 1 and 3 and approaches on 2 and 4, clean at 90 bpm.

title: Install the lean
goal: One note (open A), shuffled pairs for two minutes against the groove machine's shuffle click — your short note quieter, your long note fat, no drifting straight.

title: The shuffled boogie through the full 12 bars
goal: The R-3-5-6 boogie, shuffled, through the complete form in A at 96 — shape shifts clean at every change, feel identical in all three chords.

title: Real–ghost–real–ghost
goal: Sixteen sixteenths alternating full notes and ghosts on one fret: the fat notes identical, the ghosts pitchless thunks, the grid metronomic — at 90 bpm.

title: Protect The One
goal: The page's groove looped for two minutes: every bar's beat 1 lands huge and together with the click, no matter how the middle of the bar went.

title: Compose your own bar of funk
goal: One original bar on the 16th grid (root/8ve/♭7/5 + ghosts) written on the rhythm grid idea from Unit 1, then played clean and looped seamlessly.

title: Long quarters (the legato drill)
goal: Sixteen bars of walking where every quarter rings until the next attack — recorded, and the recording confirms zero gaps — swung, at 110.

title: Enclosures into every root
goal: The turnaround with every bar-4 approach replaced by an enclosure (above-below-land or below-above-land) at 100 bpm, landings on time.

title: Walk a full jazz blues chorus
goal: Twelve bars of walking over the blues form (dominant 7ths), swung, never repeating a bar shape twice in a row — your Unit 5 and Unit 6 skills in one take.

title: Own the empty One
goal: Two minutes over the one-drop preset resting on every beat 1 — the silence starting exactly on time, your count never stopping, no accidental early entries.

title: The five-minute phrase
goal: The page's two-bar phrase repeated for five unbroken minutes, note lengths identical every pass — one deliberate variation allowed at minute four.

title: Three minutes of eighths (the endurance cut)
goal: Root eighths through I–V–vi–IV for three full minutes: even volume, even length, alternation intact, no tempo creep — verified against the groove machine.

title: One variation per section
goal: The page's groove played four times: straight, then +fifths, then +octave lift, then +walk-ins — each variation deployed once, on purpose, at the section seam.

title: The dotted pulse through changes
goal: Root(dotted-quarter)–fifth(eighth) through I–vi–IV–V for two minutes, the '&-of-2' landing identically every bar — the era's heartbeat, automatic.

title: Chromatic connections as a lifestyle
goal: The page's groove played with EVERY chord change connected chromatically (from above or below, your choice per bar) without breaking the pulse.

title: Thumb as metronome
goal: Two minutes of muted thumb-slaps on the E string at 60 bpm — even as a drum machine, bouncing off the string, wrist loose enough to shake water off.

title: The slap-pop octave
goal: Thumb on the root (A, E-string 5… any root), pop on its octave — the classic pair, clean and even at 76 bpm, then in the disco-octave pattern from Unit 5.

title: Harmonic bell check
goal: Clear bells at frets 12, 7, and 5 on every string — touch point exact, release immediate — plus the E7/A5 tuning cross-check performed once.

title: Password decoding sprint
goal: Ten scary symbols (Dm9, G13, Cmaj9, A7alt, F6/9, E7♯9, Bm11, Ab13, D7♭9, Gadd9) each decoded in seconds: root, family (maj/min/dom), and your first-choice note.

title: One upstairs fill, placed right
goal: Over a Cmaj9 vamp: root groove for three bars, then one fill touching the 9 (D) UP an octave-plus — color without mud, landed back on the root.

title: Play the worked example with intent
goal: The 8-bar example played with function-aware choices: planted roots on I, chromatic approaches into each visitor's target, the Fm bar milked for its ache.

title: Analyze three songs you already play
goal: Three real charts marked up with numerals + functions + circled visitors, and one sentence each about what the bass should therefore do.

title: The one-fill-per-phrase discipline
goal: Eight bars looping (groove machine, any preset): solid line for 3½ bars, ONE fill into bar 5's downbeat, repeat — ten minutes, every fill landing.

title: Sing-then-play solo chorus
goal: One 12-bar chorus built phrase by phrase: sing two bars, play those two bars, until a complete sung-then-played solo exists — taste guaranteed by your own voice.

title: Four flavors over one drone
goal: Over an A drone (tuning-page style or the demo): Ionian, Mixolydian, Dorian, Aeolian played in sequence, the changed notes named out loud as they pass.

title: A Dorian vamp line
goal: Two minutes over an Am7 vamp using the Dorian 6th (F♯) as a deliberate color — placed, not stumbled on — at 94 bpm.

title: Your first full transcription
goal: One Level-1/2 song lifted completely by ear, written down in any notation, and played along with the record until the two blur.

title: The weekly steal
goal: Four consecutive weeks, one transcribed line (or riff, or fill) per week — logged here — until stealing is just something you do.

title: Four strings, one at a time
goal: Each open string rings long and clean — no buzz, no double-hit — and you name it as it sounds.
pageTitle: Session 1 · Day one: make it rumble

title: The four-string tour
goal: Sixteen even plucks — a bar each on E, A, D, then A — with every string change landing exactly on the count of 1.
pageTitle: Session 1 · Day one: make it rumble
